commonProblems.h2: Common Ford Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Ford engines dominate UK roads, but high-volume families like the 1.0 EcoBoost, 1.6 TDCi, and 2.0 TDCi have well-documented failure points that often push owners toward a repair-or-replace decision. Across the board, overheating, turbo failure, and wet-belt degradation cause the most premature replacements. Every rebuilt Ford engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Below, we break down the most common failures, what you’ll actually pay at a dealer versus a specialist, and when a full engine replacement is the only sensible option.

problems.h4: 1.0 EcoBoost Coolant Leak & Overheating – The Open-Deck Weakness
problems.affectedModels: Ford Fiesta (2012–2017, HXJA/HXJB), Ford Focus (2012–2018, M1JE/M2JE/M1DA/M2DA), Ford B-MAX (2012–2017, HXJB), Ford EcoSport (2014–2018)
problems.rootCause: The 1.0 EcoBoost uses an open-deck block design with narrow coolant passages between cylinders. Degraded coolant or air pockets cause localised overheating, leading to head gasket failure, liner fretting, and – in advanced cases – cracks in the cylinder block between cylinders 2 and 3.
problems.recommendation: If your Fiesta or Focus 1.0 EcoBoost has overheated once but passed a block test and shows no external coolant weep, a specialist head gasket replacement with a pressure test is worth attempting — but only if caught immediately. Any second overheat event, visible coolant loss without external leak, or confirmed cylinder compression cross-leak makes replacement the only financially sensible route. On a car worth £2,000–£5,000, a dealer repair often exceeds vehicle value; a specialist-fitted rebuilt engine with warranty is the smarter play.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 1.0 EcoBoost engine prices for your Fiesta or Focus

problems.h4: 1.6 TDCi (DV6) Turbo Failure & Oil Starvation – The Coked Oil Killer
problems.affectedModels: Ford Focus (2008–2015, HHJA/HHJB), Ford Fiesta (2008–2017, HHJA/HHJB), Ford C-MAX (2010–2015), Ford Ecosport (2014–2018)
problems.rootCause: The 1.6 TDCi (PSA DV6) suffers from inadequate oil drain from the turbo bearing housing. Short journeys and extended oil change intervals cook oil in the turbo feed line, which carbonises and blocks the oil supply. The turbo then runs dry, the shaft snaps, and debris is fed directly into the intake, often taking the engine with it.
problems.recommendation: The 1.6 TDCi is a disposable engine once turbo debris has circulated. If you caught the failure immediately (engine cut out, no knocking), a specialist turbo + oil system overhaul is viable. However, most drivers ignore the first signs of turbo whine. In 80% of cases we see, the bottom end has already been damaged. For a Focus or Fiesta worth £1,500–£3,000, a rebuilt engine from a specialist is the same cost as a dealer turbo repair – but it comes with warranty and upgraded oil system parts. We recommend full replacement.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt 1.6 TDCi engines with uprated turbo oil supply

problems.h4: 2.0 TDCi Wet Belt Failure (EcoBlue) – The Disintegrating Timing Belt
problems.affectedModels: Ford Transit Custom (2016–2021, 2.0 EcoBlue BJFB/BLFA/BLFB), Ford Transit (2016–2021), Ford Ranger (2016–2021, 2.0 EcoBlue), Ford Mondeo (2016–2020)
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 50,000 – 90,000 miles (commonly well before Ford’s 125,000-mile service interval)
problems.rootCause: Ford’s “wet belt” (timing belt running in engine oil) degrades due to oil contamination, soot loading, and extended service intervals. The belt surface breaks down, clogging the oil pickup strainer with rubber debris. Oil pressure drops, then the belt can snap or jump teeth, causing catastrophic valve-to-piston contact.
problems.recommendation: Do not wait for Ford’s recommended 125,000-mile interval on 2.0 EcoBlue engines. If your Transit or Ranger has passed 60,000 miles without a wet belt change, consider it an urgent preventative job. Once the belt starts shedding, the oil pickup blocks within weeks. We have seen countless vans written off by this failure. If the engine still starts and runs without knocking, the specialist minor repair is excellent value. If you’ve already lost oil pressure or the engine cut out, only a rebuilt engine will be reliable.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 2.0 EcoBlue engine prices for your Transit or Ranger

problems.h4: 1.5 & 2.0 EcoBoost Cylinder Cracking (Gen 2) – The Coolant Intrusion Problem
problems.affectedModels: Ford Focus ST (2015–2018, 2.0 EcoBoost R9DA), Ford Mondeo (2015–2020, 1.5 EcoBoost M8DB/M9DA), Ford Galaxy (2016–2020, 2.0 EcoBoost), Ford Kuga (2015–2019, 1.5 & 2.0 EcoBoost)
problems.rootCause: A manufacturing porosity issue in the cylinder block between cylinders 2 and 3 allows coolant to seep into the cylinder bore under load. Over time, this causes localised steam explosions that erode the cylinder wall, leading to coolant loss, misfire, and eventually hydro-lock or a cracked block.
problems.recommendation: This failure gives no warning before major coolant loss. On a Focus ST or Kuga worth £6,000–£12,000, a specialist-fitted rebuilt engine is almost always better value than a dealer new crate engine. Do not fit a used engine from a 2015–2018 donor vehicle – you will almost certainly inherit the same casting flaw. We only recommend a fully rebuilt engine with documented block testing or a known revised casting number.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for revised-casting 1.5 / 2.0 EcoBoost engines

problems.h4: 2.2 TDCi (Duratorq) Injector & Cylinder Head Failure – The Transit Workhorse Killer
problems.affectedModels: Ford Transit (2006–2016, 2.2 TDCi CYFA/P8FA/JXFA), Ford Ranger (2011–2016, 2.2 TDCi), Ford Mondeo (2008–2014, 2.2 TDCi)
problems.rootCause: The 2.2 TDCi’s injector clamps can lose clamping force over time, allowing combustion gasses to leak past the injector seal. This causes carbon “cupping” around the injector tip, leading to injector seizure in the cylinder head. Removal then damages the injector bore, and the cylinder head becomes scrap. Simultaneously, the EGR cooler cracks, introducing coolant into the exhaust and blocking the DPF.
problems.recommendation: For a Transit work van that earns daily, downtime is your biggest cost. If you notice a chuffing noise from an injector or a diesel smell in the oil, act immediately – a specialist injector seal replacement is cheap and prevents head damage. Once an injector seizes, the original head is scrap. On a high-mileage van, we recommend moving straight to a rebuilt engine with uprated clamps. It gives you a fresh warranty and fixed costs, avoiding a second failure six months later.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 2.2 TDCi engine prices for your Transit or Ranger

problems.group: 1.7 & 2.0 TDCi (Endura) Crankshaft Pulley & Oil Pump Drive Failure
problems.h4: 1.7 & 2.0 TDCi (Endura) Crankshaft Pulley & Oil Pump Drive Failure
problems.affectedModels: Ford Mondeo Mk4 (2007–2014, 2.0 TDCi), Ford Galaxy (2006–2015, 1.7 & 2.0 TDCi), Ford S-MAX (2006–2015)
problems.rootCause: The crankshaft pulley is secured by a friction washer and a single bolt. Over time, the friction washer loses clamping force, allowing the pulley to wobble. This damages the crankshaft nose and shears the oil pump drive tang. Oil pressure drops without warning, and the engine suffers bottom end bearing failure within seconds.
problems.recommendation: Watch for a rough idle that disappears above 1,500 rpm – that’s the first sign of pulley wobble. If you have a Mondeo or Galaxy over 90,000 miles, have a specialist inspect the crankshaft pulley at every service. The specialist minor repair is excellent value and prevents the cascade failure. However, once the oil light comes on, damage is already done. We rarely recommend an intermediate repair at that point – the crank nose is usually too far gone. Replacement with a dowelled-pulley rebuilt engine is the honest, long-term answer.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt 2.0 TDCi engines with modified crankshaft pulley

engineTypes.h2: Ford Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Ford engine type available through our UK network, with typical price ranges and recommendations based on your vehicle’s age, condition and your budget.

types.title: Ford Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected engine where all worn components are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Best for owners who want factory reliability without the factory price – ideal for common Ford diesel units with DPF or injector wear.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £1,800 – £4,200 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Ford Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild with upgraded components addressing known Ford failure points – such as improved wet belt materials for 1.0 EcoBoost and upgraded injector seals for 1.6 TDCi – for a longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Ford Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with verified history and warranty available – popular for older Fiesta, Focus and Mondeo models where a full recon exceeds vehicle value.

types.title: Ford Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for performance models (Focus ST, Fiesta ST) or owners wanting a zero‑mile Ford engine.

types.title: Ford Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and tested engine that works reliably but hasn’t had a complete rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older or budget‑sensitive Ford models (e.g., Mk6 Fiesta, Mk2 Focus) where a full recon isn’t economical.

types.title: Ford Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Ford specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting, fluids, and a workmanship warranty – particularly popular for Transit and Transit Custom owners who need a turnkey solution with minimal downtime.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £450 – £800 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Ford engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code, supplier, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Ford model today.

engineSizes.h2: Ford Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Ford engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Ford 1.5L Diesel Engines
items.description: Ford introduced the 1.5L Duratorq TDCi as a smaller-capacity diesel for light commercial applications, primarily replacing the 1.6 TDCi in Transits and vans from 2014 onward . This four-cylinder common-rail turbo diesel was designed for fuel efficiency in urban delivery work, but like the earlier 1.6 TDCi, it shares similar turbo and EGR system vulnerabilities that drive replacement demand in high-mileage fleets
items.cta: Get quotes for Ford 1.5L diesel engines

items.title: Ford 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.6L Duratorq TDCi (PSA DV6) is one of Ford’s most widely used diesel engines across passenger cars and light vans. Found in millions of Fiestas, Focuses, and Transits, this four- cylinder unit offers strong fuel economy but suffers from a critical design flaw in the turbo oil feed system . The engine’s oil drain cokes on short journeys, starving the turbo and often sending debris through the engine – a failure pattern that makes replacement more common than repair
items.cta: Get quotes for Ford 1.6L diesel engines
commonFailurePoints: Turbo oil starvation and shaft failure

items.title: Ford 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.0L Duratorq TDCi and later EcoBlue represent Ford’s workhorse diesel for medium and large applications. From the Mk4 Mondeo to the Transit Custom, this four-cylinder common-rail engine comes in multiple power outputs. The earlier Duratorq (2008–2016) suffers from crankshaft pulley and injector clamp failures, while the later EcoBlue (2016 onwards) introduced a “wet belt” timing system that sheds rubber debris, blocking the oil pickup and causing catastrophic engine failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Ford 2.0L diesel engines
commonFailurePoints: EcoBlue wet belt disintegration (oil pump blockage)

items.title: Ford 2.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.2L Duratorq TDCi (also branded as Powerstroke in some markets) is Ford’s heavy-duty four-cylinder diesel for full-size Transits, Rangers, and commercial chassis cabs . Known internally as the “Puma” engine family (derived from the earlier PSA DW12), this unit delivers torque suitable for 3.5-tonne vans and towing. Production years from 2006 to 2016 cover millions of Transit vans, and injector seizure in the cylinder head remains the most common reason for replacement – often requiring a full head or complete engine
items.cta: Get quotes for Ford 2.2L diesel engines

items.title: Ford 2.5L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.5L Duratorq TDCi is a less common five-cylinder diesel used primarily in Ford Ranger pickups before the 2.2L and 3.2L units took over. This engine is essentially a detuned version of the 3.2L five-cylinder, sharing its turbocharged common-rail architecture. In the UK market, it appeared mainly in the T6-generation Ranger (pre-facelift) but was quickly phased out in favour of the 2.2L four-cylinder for tax efficiency and the more powerful 3.2L for premium trims
items.cta: Get quotes for Ford 2.5L diesel engines

items.title: Ford 3.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 3.2L Duratorq TDCi (marketed as Powerstroke in North America) is Ford’s most powerful five-cylinder diesel, used in heavy-duty Ranger Wildtrak models and Transit vans . With 200PS and 470Nm, this engine was designed for maximum towing and load capacity. In the UK, it’s most associated with the Ranger Wildtrak (2012–2022) and high-spec Transits. The five-cylinder layout gives it a distinctive sound, but EGR cooler failures and DPF clogging are common high-mileage issues – and replacement engines remain in strong demand due to their commercial value
items.cta: Get quotes for Ford 3.2L diesel engines

items.title: Ford 1.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.0L EcoBoost is Ford’s most famous small petrol engine – a three-cylinder turbocharged unit that won multiple International Engine of the Year awards. Naturally aspirated versions (Fox) also exist in base-spec Fiestas . The 1.0L displaced 998cc and used a cast-iron block with an integrated exhaust manifold. Despite its acclaim, the engine has a well-documented Achilles’ heel: the open-deck block design is prone to coolant loss and cracking between cylinders 2 and 3 after overheating, leading to complete replacement being the most common outcome – especially given the cost of specialist repair
commonFailurePoints: Coolant loss and cylinder block cracking (overheating)
wet belt degradation (timing belt in oil)

items.title: Ford 1.1L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.1L Duratec (Dragon family) is a naturally aspirated three-cylinder petrol engine introduced as a replacement for the 1.0L non-turbo Fox unit . With 1084cc displacement, it offered slightly more torque for city cars like the later Fiesta. Unlike the turbocharged EcoBoost engines, this unit is simpler and generally more reliable, but the naturally aspirated design means lower power output – making it less common in replacement searches as owners often upgrade to the 1.0 EcoBoost when engines fail at high mileage
engineCodes: (Dragon family – consult supplier for full list)
commonFailurePoints: carbon build-up on intake valves (direct injection)

items.title: Ford 1.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.2L Dragon engine is a naturally aspirated three-cylinder unit used in European Ford models, primarily the Fiesta and its derivatives . Displacing 1194cc, it sits between the 1.1L and 1.5L Dragon engines in Ford’s naturally aspirated lineup. This engine shares the Dragon family’s Ti-VCT variable valve timing and is generally considered robust, but replacement demand is low compared to turbocharged units – most failures are ancillary (cooling system, ignition) rather than catastrophic internal failure
engineCodes: (Dragon family – consult supplier for full list)
commonFailurePoints: ignition coil packs (less common than EcoBoost failures)

items.title: Ford 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5L Duratec (Dragon family) is available in both naturally aspirated and EcoBoost turbocharged forms . The naturally aspirated 1.5L (1497cc) was used in base-model Fiestas, while the 1.5L EcoBoost three-cylinder turbo appeared in Focus and Mondeo models as a replacement for the earlier 1.6 EcoBoost. The turbocharged version suffers from similar coolant intrusion issues to the larger 1.6L and 2.0L EcoBoost engines – a known block porosity problem that can allow coolant to seep into cylinders, often requiring a full replacement
engineCodes: various EcoBoost codes (consult supplier for turbo variants)

items.title: Ford 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6L Duratec (Sigma family) and 1.6L EcoBoost are two very different engines sharing the same displacement. The naturally aspirated 1.6 Duratec (1596cc) is a reliable four-cylinder found in millions of Mk2 Focuses and Mk6 Fiestas. The 1.6 EcoBoost (also 1596cc) added a turbocharger for performance models like the Focus ST (150/182PS) . The turbo version has a reputation for cylinder head cracking, block porosity, and coolant loss – failures that are often catastrophic and push owners toward a replacement rather than repair

items.title: Ford 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.8L Duratec (Zetec-derived) was Ford’s mid-range naturally aspirated petrol engine in the 2000s, displacing 1796cc or 1798cc depending on the generation. Found in Focus, Mondeo, and Transit Connect models, this four-cylinder engine is known for its simplicity and durability compared to the smaller EcoBoost units. However, replacement demand still exists – primarily from neglected timing belt changes (interference engine) or cooling system failures leading to head gasket issues
commonFailurePoints: coil pack failures on higher-mileage units

items.title: Ford 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0L Duratec and 2.0L EcoBoost represent Ford’s most diverse petrol displacement, spanning naturally aspirated economy units, performance ST engines, and flagship Focus RS variants . The 2.0L EcoBoost (1999cc) is particularly common in Mk3 Focus ST, Kuga, and Mondeo – and shares the same cylinder block porosity issues as the 1.5 and 1.6 EcoBoost engines. Coolant intrusion leading to hydrolock or block cracking is the number one driver of replacement demand, making rebuilt engines with revised castings the recommended solution over used units
commonFailurePoints: Coolant intrusion into cylinders (EcoBoost head/block casting issues)
carbon build-up on intake valves (direct injection)

items.title: Ford 2.3L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.3L EcoBoost is Ford’s performance four-cylinder turbo engine, evolving from the 2.0L EcoBoost with a longer stroke and larger turbocharger . Displacing 2261cc (or 2294cc in earlier versions), it’s most famous as the engine in the Focus RS Mk3 – delivering up to 350PS. In the UK, the 2.3L EcoBoost also appears in Mustang EcoBoost models. The engine is generally robust, but the RS version suffered from head gasket failures due to a production error (wrong gasket fitted), leading to coolant leaks and potential engine seizure. Replacement demand remains strong for these performance units
commonFailurePoints: Head gasket failure (Focus RS – incorrect gasket part)
piston ring land failure in tuned engines

items.title: Ford 2.5L Petrol Engines (Duratec & Turbo)
items.description: The 2.5L Duratec (originally developed with Mazda) and the 2.5L Turbo (Volvo-derived) have very different characters. The naturally aspirated 2.5L (2488cc, known as MZR/CD player) appeared in the Ford Galaxy, S-MAX, and Mondeo as a V6 replacement. More famously, the 2.5L Turbo five-cylinder (from Volvo) powered the Focus ST225 (2005–2010) . This five-cylinder unit – with its distinctive warble – produces 225PS and is highly tunable. Replacement demand is driven by high-mileage wear (turbo seals, PCV system) rather than catastrophic failure, but performance enthusiasts still seek rebuilt units
commonFailurePoints: PCV system clogs causing crankcase pressure

items.title: Ford 3.0L Petrol Engines (V6 Duratec)
items.description: The 3.0L Duratec V6 (originally developed by Mazda and Ford) was Ford’s flagship naturally aspirated V6 in the 1990s and 2000s . Displacing 2967cc, this 60-degree V6 powered the Mondeo ST220 (223PS), Ford Taurus, and Lincoln LS. In the UK, it’s almost exclusively remembered for the Mondeo ST220 – a cult classic that still generates replacement demand for high-mileage or overheated units. The engine is robust, but water pump failure can lead to overheating and head gasket issues, while variable timing components (VCT) wear on higher-mileage cars

engineCodeDirectory.h2: Ford Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Ford engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Ford specialists.

entries.title: HXJA / HXJB / H1JE 1.0L Petrol
entries.description: Used across Fiesta (2013–2019), Focus (2013–2018), B‑Max, C‑Max, and EcoSport. The 1.0 EcoBoost is notorious for wet belt (timing belt in oil) degradation, where belt fibres shed and block the oil pickup strainer, leading to oil starvation and turbo or bottom‑end failure. Rebuilt units include a full timing belt kit, new oil pump, uprated belt materials where available, and a thorough oil system flush.
entries.cta: Get quotes for HXJA/HXJB Ford engine replacement

entries.title: M1JE / M2JE / M7JE 1.0L Petrol
entries.description: Early 1.0 EcoBoost codes fitted to Fiesta (2012–2017), Focus (2012–2018), and B‑Max. These engines share the same wet belt weakness as later HXJA codes but also suffer from coolant hose brittleness and thermostat housing cracks. Replacement is often more cost‑effective than repair once oil pressure drops. Quality rebuilt units come with a new wet belt kit, water pump, and upgraded coolant pipework.
entries.cta: Compare M1JE/M2JE Ford engine prices

entries.title: SFJA / SFJB 1.0L Petrol
entries.description: Late‑production 1.0 EcoBoost codes (Fiesta 2016–2020, Focus 2015–2018, Puma 2019–2020). While Ford made minor revisions, wet belt failure remains the primary replacement driver at 60,000–90,000 miles. Specialist rebuilt units include the latest belt spec, revised oil pump, and all associated tensioners – plus a mandatory 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty.
entries.cta: Get quotes for SFJA Ford engine replacement

entries.title: YJDA / YJDB / YJDC 1.5L Petrol
entries.description: Fitted to Focus Mk4 (2018+), Mondeo Mk5, and Kuga Mk2. Known for coolant intrusion between cylinders (caused by block casting weaknesses in early production) and high‑pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure. Rebuilt engines include a revised cylinder head gasket, new HPFP, and full carbon cleaning of intake valves.
entries.cta: Compare YJDA Ford engine prices

entries.title: R9DA / R9DB 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Found in Focus ST (2019+), Kuga ST‑Line, and Mondeo Vignale (240–250 PS). Common failures include HPFP failure, carbon buildup on intake valves, and VCT solenoid sticking. A quality rebuild includes new HPFP, timing chain kit, VCT units, and walnut blasting of intake ports.
entries.cta: Get quotes for R9DA/R9DB Ford engine replacement

entries.title: N9DA / N9DB / N9DG 2.3L Petrol
entries.description: Powers Focus ST (2019+), Mustang EcoBoost (2015–2017), and Explorer. Replacement demand arises from HPFP failure, wastegate issues on the turbocharger, and oil consumption from blocked piston rings. Rebuilt units include new HPFP, turbo cartridge inspection/replacement, and piston ring replacement where indicated.
entries.cta: Compare 2.3L Ford EcoBoost engine prices

entries.title: HHJA / HHJB / HHJC 1.6L Diesel
entries.description: Among the most searched diesel codes for Fiesta (2009–2015), Focus (2005–2011), and Fusion. Notorious for turbocharger failure (variable vanes sticking) and injector copper seal blow‑by, which erodes the injector seat and dilutes engine oil. Rebuilt engines include a new or reconditioned turbo, all four injectors with new seals, and cylinder head injector seat recutting.
entries.cta: Get quotes for HHJA Ford engine replacement

entries.title: G8DA / G8DB / G8DD / G8DE 1.6L Diesel
entries.description: Fitted to Focus (2008–2015), C‑Max (2007–2014), and Transit Connect (2009–2014). Same turbo and injector seal weaknesses as HHJA, plus DPF clogging on higher‑mileage units. Rebuilt engines come with a recon turbo, refurbished injectors, DPF cleaning, and a full timing belt kit.
entries.cta: Compare G8DA/G8DB Ford engine prices

entries.title: UBJA / H9DA 1.6L Diesel
entries.description: Later 1.6 TDCi variant (Focus 2011–2015, B‑Max 2012–2017). Introduced minor refinements, but turbo failure and injector seat wear remain common beyond 80,000 miles. A rebuilt unit with upgraded injector seals and a turbo service kit offers better value than repeated repairs.
entries.cta: Get quotes for UBJA Ford diesel engine replacement

entries.title: AZBA / AZBC 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: One of the most‑replaced Ford diesel codes, fitted to Mondeo Mk4 (2007–2015), Galaxy, and S‑Max. Known for DPF clogging, injector failure, and EGR valve sticking. Rebuilt engines include new or refurbished injectors, DPF cleaning, EGR replacement, and a full timing belt kit.
entries.cta: Compare AZBA Ford engine prices

entries.title: BWBA / BWBB / C7DB 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Used in Mondeo (2010–2014), Galaxy, and S‑Max (140–150 PS). Common replacement drivers include dual‑mass flywheel failure (indirectly affecting engine balance), injector seal leaks, and turbo sticking. Rebuilt units include a new DMF (on request), recon injectors, turbo inspection, and full oil system flush.
entries.cta: Get quotes for BWBA Ford engine replacement

entries.title: UFBA / UGBA / UHBA 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Later 2.0 TDCi variant for Focus (2011–2015), Kuga (2013–2019), and Mondeo (2011–2014). Suffers from EGR cooler failure, DPF clogging, and injector deviation issues. Specialist rebuilt units include EGR cooler replacement, DPF clean, and all four injectors tested and recalibrated.
entries.cta: Compare UFBA/UGBA Ford diesel engine prices

entries.title: TYBA / TYBB / SZBA 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Fitted to Focus (2008–2015) and Mondeo (2007–2014). Replacement demand is driven by injector failure and DPF blockage at 100,000+ miles. A rebuilt engine with new injectors and DPF is often more cost‑effective than paying for piecemeal repairs plus labour.
entries.cta: Get quotes for TYBA Ford engine replacement

entries.title: BJFA / BJFB / BKFA / BKFB 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: (103–130 PS) Widely used in Transit Custom (2016–2022) and Transit (2016–2022). Common failures include wet belt degradation (timing belt in oil) and injector clogging. Rebuilt units include a full wet belt kit, new injectors, oil pump, and a complete oil system flush. Commercial warranty (12 months) included.
entries.cta: Get quotes for BJFA/BJFB Ford engine replacement

entries.title: BLFA / BLFB 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: (167–170 PS) Higher‑power variant for Transit Custom and Transit. Suffers from the same wet belt issues as BJFA, plus occasional HPFP failure. Replacement is the preferred route when oil starvation has reached the turbo or crank bearings. Rebuilt engines include an uprated belt kit, HPFP inspection, and new injectors.
entries.cta: Compare BLFA/BLFB Ford engine prices

entries.title: JXFA / JXFB / D2FA 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: (170 PS) Fitted to Transit Custom and Ranger (2019–2022). Known for injector failure and wet belt debris blocking the oil pickup. A quality rebuild includes new injectors, a full wet belt kit (with uprated belt material), oil pump, and bench testing before delivery.
entries.cta: Get quotes for JXFA Ford engine replacement

entries.title: CYRA / P8FA / SRFA 2.2L Diesel
entries.description: Used in Transit (2011–2014) and Ranger (2011–2015). Common failures include injector seizure, crankshaft pulley wear, and EGR cooler cracks. Rebuilt units include new injectors, EGR cooler, and a full gasket and seal kit.
entries.cta: Compare CYRA/P8FA Ford engine prices

entries.title: BCFA / BCFB 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: (185 PS – mHEV) Higher‑output mild‑hybrid EcoBlue for Transit Custom and Tourneo Custom (2019–2024). Shares the wet belt weakness of earlier EcoBlue engines, but with added complexity from the belt‑integrated starter generator (BiSG). Specialist rebuilt units include the full hybrid belt system, injectors, and a 12‑month commercial warranty.
entries.cta: Get quotes for BCFA Ford engine replacement

entries.title: FUJA / FUJB / FXJA / FXJB 1.25L / 1.4L Petrol
entries.description: Fitted to Fiesta (1995–2008) and Fusion (2002–2012). Very reliable, but replacement demand arises from timing chain stretch (100,000+ miles), head gasket failure, and valve seat wear. Rebuilt units include a new timing chain kit, cylinder head skim, and valve seat recutting.
entries.cta: Compare FUJA/FXJA Ford petrol engine prices

entries.title: HWJA / IQJA / PZJA 1.6L Petrol
entries.description: (Duratec / Ti‑VCT) Used across Fiesta, Focus, C‑Max, and Fusion (2004–2015). Also includes 1.6 EcoBoost (JQJA / JTJA) for Focus ST (2012–2015). Common failures include timing chain stretch, VCT solenoid issues, and HPFP failure on direct‑injection variants. Rebuilt units include a new timing chain, VCT units, and HPFP where fitted. engineYears.h2: Ford Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section covers Ford's complete UK engine timeline from 1998 to the present across every major model line - from the Zetec and Endura petrol families and the landmark Duratorq TDCi diesel through the EcoBoost turbocharged revolution, the 1.0 EcoBoost wet belt controversy, the 2.0 EcoBlue diesel generation, and the current Mustang Mach-E and Puma hybrid era - so owners can identify exactly what was fitted to their vehicle by year and compare rebuilt Ford engine prices from UK specialists.

years.preview: Zetec & Endura Petrol Peak, Duratorq TDDi Introduction & Focus Generation One Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Ford's late 1990s UK range was built around the Zetec 1.6/1.8/2.0 DOHC petrol and the Endura-E 1.3/1.4 entry units, while the new Focus (1998) and Mondeo TDDi introduced Ford's first turbodiesel direct-injection architecture to the volume car range.
keyChanges: Ford Focus first generation launched (1998) with Zetec-SE 1.4/1.6 and 1.8/2.0 DOHC petrol - replaces Escort
Mondeo Mk3 previewed (2000) - Duratec HE 2.0 petrol and Duratorq TDDi/TDCi diesel preparation
Duratorq TDDi 1.8 (DLD-418) introduced in Focus (1999) and Fiesta - Ford's first direct-injection diesel in a small car 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
Fiesta continues with Endura-E 1.25/1.3/1.4 and Zetec-SE petrol
Galaxy launched with VW Group 1.9 TDI and 2.8 V6 (joint venture)
Euro 2/3 compliance in force
knownFor: Zetec 1.8/2.0 is a genuinely durable, well-regarded DOHC petrol engine - cam cover oil leaks and coolant temperature sensor faults are the most common UK specialist concerns on high-mileage examples but otherwise long-lived and easy to rebuild

Duratorq TDDi 1.8 in Focus and Mondeo is Ford's first step into direct-injection diesel volume - the non-common-rail TDDi unit is notably less refined than later TDCi but very durable; timing belt failure at 80,000–100,000 miles is the primary concern
Endura-E 1.25 in Fiesta generates low replacement demand - a simple, robust unit widely regarded as close to indestructible at typical Fiesta mileages
Galaxy 1.9 TDI uses VW Group PD diesel - shares all the timing belt concerns of VW's own unit; the joint-venture platform generates cross-brand specialist knowledge
years.cta: Find rebuilt 1998–2001 Ford Zetec and TDDi engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Mondeo Mk3, Focus RS & Duratorq TDCi Common-Rail Launch Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The Mondeo Mk3 and updated Focus brought Ford's common-rail Duratorq TDCi diesel to mass-market UK buyers for the first time - one of the most commercially significant Ford engine introductions since the Zetec - while the Focus RS delivered the highest-performance Ford hatchback of its era.
keyChanges: Mondeo Mk3 (2000 global, UK volume 2002) with Duratec HE 2.0 petrol and Duratorq TDCi 2.0 FMBA/HJBA - first common-rail Ford diesel in Mondeo 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
Focus RS first generation (2002) - Duratec RS 2.0 turbocharged 212 hp; first Focus performance flagship 🔴 Performance Landmark
Focus 1.6 TDCi introduced (2003) - PSA-co-developed DV6/DLD-416 diesel; 109 hp
Fiesta 1.4 TDCi introduced (2003) - DLD-414; smallest Ford common-rail diesel for UK market
Transit updated with Duratorq TDCi 2.4 engine across van range
Euro 3 dominant; Euro 4 preparation
mainEngines: HJBA / FMBA - 2.0 Duratorq TDCi 130 hp (Mondeo, S-Max early preview)
Duratec HE 2.0 - naturally aspirated petrol (Mondeo, Focus 2.0)
knownFor: 2.0 Duratorq TDCi in Mondeo Mk3 is widely considered the best Ford diesel of this period - early common-rail architecture, strong torque, relatively simple compared to later units; injector wear from poor fuel quality and EGR carbon fouling are the primary high-mileage concerns

Focus RS Duratec 2.0T is a landmark performance engine - 212 hp from a naturally aspirated architecture with turbocharger; head gasket failure under sustained hard use is the one significant documented weakness on early examples

1.6 TDCi DLD-416 (PSA co-developed) is considered the weakest of the three Duratorq diesel families - turbocharger oil-feed pipe blockage causing turbo failure from 70,000 miles is the dominant UK specialist complaint; injector and EGR issues compound the reputation

2.4 Duratorq TDCi in Transit is the single highest-volume Ford replacement diesel in UK history at this point - thermal detonation causing piston failure and injector failure are the two primary rebuild triggers

years.cta: Compare 2002–2004 Ford Mondeo, Focus, and Transit engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: S-Max & Galaxy, Focus Mk2, Duratec 2.5T & 2.2 TDCi Introduction Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The S-Max and Galaxy MPV arrivals brought the 2.0 TDCi and Duratec 2.5 turbocharged five-cylinder to new family car buyers, the Focus Mk2 established the 1.6 TDCi and 1.6 Duratec Ti-VCT as the UK's most common Ford pairing, and the 2.2 TDCi introduced a new performance diesel tier.
keyChanges: S-Max / Galaxy second generation (2006) - Duratec 2.0, 2.5T five-cylinder, and 2.0 TDCi UKWA/QXWB diesel 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
Duratec 2.5T turbocharged five-cylinder (HYDA/JQDA) introduced - 220 hp; Focus ST and S-Max 🔴 Performance Landmark
Focus Mk2 (2004 launch, UK volume 2005) - 1.4/1.6/1.8/2.0 Duratec Ti-VCT petrol and 1.6/1.8/2.0 TDCi
2.2 TDCi (QJBA) introduced in S-Max, Mondeo - higher-displacement diesel for executive and load-carrying applications
Kuga first generation previewed (2007) - 2.0 TDCi diesel and 2.5T petrol platform
Euro 4 enforced
mainEngines: HYDA / JQDA - 2.5T Duratec five-cylinder 220 hp (Focus ST, S-Max 2.5T)
QXWB / UKWA - 2.0 TDCi 130/140 hp (Mondeo, S-Max, Galaxy)
knownFor: 2.5T Duratec five-cylinder in Focus ST and S-Max is a characterful, high-torque engine - smooth and powerful; timing belt failure remains the primary maintenance-critical item; boost hose split at the intercooler is a common nuisance fault on high-mileage examples

2.2 TDCi in Mondeo and S-Max is considered the UK's most problematic Ford diesel of this generation - injector quality described by diesel specialists as "close to cheese" on real-world accounts; dual mass flywheel failure, EGR fouling, and fuel pump wear compound the ownership experience

Focus Mk2 1.6 TDCi PSA-sourced remains commercially significant but maintains its turbo failure reputation - UK specialist community consistently recommends oil change at 5,000-mile intervals to protect the turbocharger oil-feed circuit

2.0 TDCi in S-Max and Mondeo from 2003–2007 (QXWB/UKWA spec) is considered the most reliable of the Duratorq diesel range for this era by UK independent specialists - regular oil changes and premium diesel are key

years.cta: Get 2005–2007 Ford S-Max, Mondeo, and Focus engine replacement quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: Kuga, Transit 2.2 TDCi, Mondeo Mk4 & Euro 5 Transition
years.description: The Kuga SUV arrived with the 2.0 TDCi as Ford's first dedicated compact SUV for UK buyers, the Transit updated to a higher-specification 2.2 TDCi unit, and the Mondeo Mk4 brought revised Duratorq TDCi and Duratec engines ahead of the EcoBoost era.
keyChanges: Kuga first generation (2008) - 2.0 TDCi UFBA/TXBA diesel and 2.5T petrol; Ford's first UK compact SUV
Transit updated (2006–2013 generation dominant from 2008) - 2.2 TDCi (DRFA/CYFA) and 2.4 TDCi replacing previous units
Mondeo Mk4 (2007 launch, UK volume 2008–2009) - 1.6 TDCi, 2.0 TDCi UFBA, 2.0 EcoBoost preview
Focus Mk2 continues - 1.6 TDCi, 2.0 TDCi, 1.6 Ti-VCT
Fiesta sixth generation (2008) with Duratec 1.25/1.4/1.6 and 1.4/1.6 TDCi
Euro 5 preparation begins from 2009
mainEngines: UFBA / TXBA - 2.0 TDCi 136/140 hp (Kuga, Mondeo Mk4, Focus estate)
SHDA / QQDB - 1.8/2.0 Duratec Ti-VCT (Mondeo, Focus Mk2 petrol)
knownFor: Transit 2.2 TDCi is the most widely replaced Ford commercial engine in UK history - thermal detonation leading to piston failure, faulty injectors, and DPF contamination from diesel-contaminated oil are the three core failure modes cited by leading Ford diesel remanufacturers
Mondeo Mk4 2.0 TDCi (UFBA) recalled for sump cracking risk due to ECU overheating detection fault - 48,595 UK vehicles affected; a well-documented production concern that predates the EcoBoost era

Kuga 2.0 TDCi 4WD is well-regarded in the UK independent specialist community - capable and reliable diesel but EGR valve and DPF blockage on urban-use examples are the predictable maintenance concerns from 60,000 miles

Fiesta sixth generation 1.25 Duratec continues to build a near-bulletproof reliability reputation - this engine in Fiesta generates virtually no replacement engine demand, one of the lowest failure-rate engines in the Ford UK fleet

years.cta: Compare 2008–2011 Ford Transit, Kuga, and Mondeo engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: EcoBoost Revolution: 1.0, 1.6 & 2.0 EcoBoost & Focus Mk3 Major Engine Family Introduction Performance Landmark
years.description: 2012 is the single most transformative year in Ford's modern UK engine history - the 1.0 EcoBoost three-cylinder turbocharged petrol won International Engine of the Year three times consecutively and rewrote the rules for small-displacement performance, arriving alongside the 1.6 and 2.0 EcoBoost across Focus, Mondeo, and Kuga.
keyChanges: 1.0 EcoBoost (Fox/SFDA/SFDB) launched (2012) - three-cylinder 100/125 hp; International Engine of the Year 2012, 2013, 2014; wet timing belt in oil 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction 🔴 Performance Landmark
1.6 EcoBoost (JQMA/JQMB) - 150/182 hp (Focus, Mondeo, Kuga, S-Max)
2.0 EcoBoost (TNWA/TPWA) - 240/250 hp (Mondeo, S-Max, Kuga 2.0T)
Focus Mk3 (2011 launch, UK dominant 2012) - 1.0 EcoBoost, 1.6 EcoBoost, 2.0 TDCi; 1.6 Ti-VCT continues
Focus ST Mk3 (2012) - 2.0 EcoBoost 247 hp 🔴 Performance Landmark
Kuga second generation (2013) - 1.6 EcoBoost, 2.0 EcoBoost, 2.0 TDCi
Euro 5 compliance dominant
mainEngines: SFDA / SFDB / M1DA - 1.0 EcoBoost three-cylinder 100/125 hp (Fiesta, Focus, C-Max, Mondeo, B-Max)
JQMA / JQMB - 1.6 EcoBoost 150/182 hp (Focus, Mondeo, Kuga)
TNWA / TPWA - 2.0 EcoBoost 240/250 hp (Focus ST, Mondeo, S-Max)
T1DA / T1DB - 2.0 TDCi (Focus Mk3, Mondeo, Kuga second gen)
knownFor: 1.0 EcoBoost wet timing belt system is technically brilliant but presents a significant long-term maintenance risk - the belt runs immersed in engine oil and degrades at accelerated rates if oil changes are neglected or incorrect-spec oil is used; belt degradation sheds rubber particles that block oil passages and can cause complete engine failure

Ford officially classifies the 1.0 EcoBoost wet belt as sealed-for-life - UK independent specialists strongly disagree and recommend proactive inspection or replacement at 60,000 miles or 6 years; this single item generates the highest volume of Ford EcoBoost engine replacement enquiries in the UK

1.6 EcoBoost JQMA/JQMB coolant loss from a poorly sealed cylinder head joint is a documented early fault - coolant enters the combustion chamber causing hydrolock or overheat damage; a widely reported failure mode across Focus and Kuga 1.6 EcoBoost from 2012–2015 production

2.0 EcoBoost in Focus ST is an impressive performance unit - strong torque, good reliability record at standard power levels; timing chain tensioner noise and high oil consumption from piston ring wear are the two most reported UK specialist concerns

years.preview: Mondeo Mk5, Mustang UK Launch, Edge & 2.0 EcoBlue Preview Performance Landmark
years.description: The Mondeo Mk5 brought the full EcoBoost petrol range to Ford's executive saloon/estate, the Mustang arrived in the UK for the first time with the 2.3 EcoBoost and 5.0 Coyote V8, and the Edge introduced the 2.0 EcoBoost to Ford's SUV range - while the diesel successor to the ageing Duratorq TDCi was in development.
keyChanges: Mondeo Mk5 (2015) - 1.0 EcoBoost, 1.5 EcoBoost, 2.0 EcoBoost, 2.0 TDCi; first Mondeo with PHEV later announced
Mustang first UK RHD generation (2015) - 2.3 EcoBoost (GTDI 317 hp) and 5.0 Coyote V8 (421 hp) 🔴 Performance Landmark
Edge launched (2015 UK) - 2.0 EcoBoost and 2.0 TDCi diesel SUV
Focus RS Mk3 (2016) - 2.3 EcoBoost 350 hp; Focus RS heritage continues 🔴 Performance Landmark
1.5 EcoBoost (M8MA/YYDA) introduced - four-cylinder replacing 1.6 EcoBoost in Focus, Mondeo, C-Max
Transit Custom updated with 2.0 EcoBlue diesel previewed - first use of EcoBlue architecture

faq.h2: Ford Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Ford owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Ford engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A replacement Ford engine typically costs between £1,500 and £3,500 for a fully reconditioned unit from a specialist, depending on the model. A common 1.6 TDCi for a Focus or Fiesta lands around £1,800–£2,500 supplied only, while a 2.0 EcoBlue for a Transit Custom runs £3,000–£4,500. Fitting adds £500–£800 for a standard swap. The best value is almost always a rebuilt engine with a 12‑month warranty, rather than a used unit of unknown history. Compare Ford engine replacement prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Ford engine replacement?
items.answer: Four main factors drive the price on any Ford: engine size (1.0 EcoBoost costs less than 3.2 TDCi), condition (used vs reconditioned vs rebuilt), vehicle complexity (Transit takes longer than Fiesta), and supply‑only vs supply‑and‑fit. Labour rates vary by location — expect 20–30% higher prices in London and the South East compared to the Midlands. Our comparison tool shows exactly what's included, so you're never surprised by hidden fees. Get quotes from UK Ford engine specialists

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Ford?
items.answer: In most cases, yes — especially if the body, chassis, and gearbox are in good shape. A rebuilt Ford engine with upgraded components (revised timing chains, updated gaskets, improved oil pump) often outlasts the original. For a car worth £1,500–£5,000, spending £2,000 on a reconditioned engine makes more financial sense than buying another used vehicle with unknown history. If your Ford is a commercial van earning daily income, replacement is almost always the right call. Compare prices for reconditioned Ford engines
items.cta: Get a Ford engine quote now

items.question: What's included in a Ford engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full supply‑and‑fit service covers everything: sourcing the correct Ford engine code, removing your failed engine, installing the replacement, all new fluids and filters, a workmanship warranty (typically 12 months), and disposal of the old unit. Most specialists also include a test‑drive and final inspection. Fitting alone typically costs £500–£800 on a standard Ford, while supply‑and‑fit packages often bundle the engine at a discounted fitted price. Find Ford engine supply and fit deals near you
items.cta: Get a Ford engine quote now

items.question: How long does a Ford engine replacement take?
items.answer: On common models like a Ford Focus, Fiesta, or Mondeo, a straightforward engine swap takes 3–5 working days from removal to road‑test. More complex Fords — Transit Custom, Ranger, or any Kuga with four‑wheel drive — can take 5–7 days due to extra components. The biggest variable is engine availability; our network of UK suppliers stocks the most commonly replaced Ford codes (1.0 EcoBoost, 1.6 TDCi, 2.0 EcoBlue) for next‑day delivery. Ask your shortlisted suppliers for exact lead times. Compare Ford engine replacement lead times
items.cta: Get a Ford engine quote now

items.question: Do reconditioned Ford engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes — every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty as standard. Many specialist rebuilders offer extended 24‑month coverage on fully reconditioned units with new timing components, pistons, rings, and bearings. Warranty typically covers major internal failures (crankshaft, conrods, pistons, timing chain, oil pump) but excludes ancillary parts like injectors, turbos, or sensors. Always confirm exact terms directly with your chosen supplier before purchase. Find reconditioned Ford engines with comprehensive warranty cover

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Ford engine?
items.answer: A used Ford engine is cheaper upfront (£600–£1,200 for common codes) and works as a short‑term fix if you're selling the car soon. A reconditioned engine (£1,500–£3,500) includes new piston rings, bearings, seals, gaskets, and often upgraded components that address the original failure point. For any Ford with known issues — 1.0 EcoBoost (coolant leaks), 1.6 TDCi (turbo failure), or 2.0 EcoBlue (wet belt) — a reconditioned unit with warranty is strongly recommended. Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Ford engine makes financial sense

items.question: What are the most common Ford engine failures?
items.answer: Four failures dominate Ford replacement demand: 1.0 EcoBoost coolant loss and block cracking (Fiesta/Focus, 2012–2017), 1.6 TDCi turbo oil starvation (Fiesta/Focus, 2008–2015), 2.0 EcoBlue wet belt disintegration (Transit Custom/Ranger, 2016–2021), and 2.2 TDCi injector seizure in cylinder head (Transit Mk7, 2006–2016). In every case, early warning signs are subtle, and once catastrophic failure occurs, a full replacement is often more cost‑effective than repeated repairs. Check your Ford engine code for known failure patterns

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Ford engine?
items.answer: Compare at least three quotes — prices on the same Ford engine code can vary by £1,000+ between suppliers. A used engine suits a tight budget or short‑term need; a reconditioned engine with upgraded components is better for long‑term ownership. Always ask whether fitting, delivery, and VAT are included in the quoted price. Some suppliers offer a discount on supply‑only if you have your own trusted garage.
